title: Hemifacial Microsomia: OMENS-Plus, Distraction, and TJR
subtitle: Block F — Congenital and Developmental Conditions
guidelines_version: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 · European Craniofacial Society Consensus 2021 · ACPA Parameters of Care 2018
textbook_refs: Pruzansky S. 'Not all dwarfed mandibles are alike.' Birth Defects (1969)
Vento AR et al. 'The O.M.E.N.S. classification of hemifacial microsomia.' Cleft Palate Craniofac J (1991)
Kaban LB et al. 'Skeletal reconstruction of the mandible using distraction osteogenesis.' J Oral Maxillofac Surg (1998)
Proffit WR et al. Contemporary Orthodontics 5th ed. (Mosby 2012)
Lauritzen C et al. 'The Stockholm protocol for hemifacial microsomia.' Scand J Plast Reconstr Surg (2001)
learning_objectives: Define hemifacial microsomia and describe the spectrum of the condition using OMENS-Plus classification
Classify mandibular hypoplasia in hemifacial microsomia using the Pruzansky-Kaban system

Evaluate early distraction osteogenesis versus late orthognathic surgery in hemifacial microsomia management, differentiating indications by Pruzansky-Kaban grade, airway status, and age, and critically appraising the evidence for each approach

Analyze the indications, surgical technique, and outcomes of total joint replacement (TJR) in severe hemifacial microsomia (Pruzansky-Kaban type III), differentiating stock from custom prostheses and evaluating evidence for growth in the operated side

Evaluate and recommend the multidisciplinary management plan from birth to skeletal maturity in a child with HFM by integrating surgical timing, orthodontic intervention, speech therapy, and the impact of treatment decisions on long-term skeletal and facial outcomes

blocks.content:
**Hemifacial microsomia (HFM)** — also known as oculo-auriculo-vertebral (OAV) spectrum, Goldenhar syndrome (when vertebral anomalies and epibulbar dermoids are present), and first and second branchial arch syndrome — is the second most common congenital craniofacial anomaly after cleft lip and palate. The condition is characterised by asymmetric underdevelopment of structures derived from the first and second branchial arches: mandible, maxilla, ear (auricle and middle ear), facial nerve, masticatory muscles, parotid gland, and soft tissues of the face.

HFM affects approximately 1 in 3,500 to 1 in 5,600 live births. The majority of cases (approximately 90%) are unilateral; bilateral involvement occurs in approximately 10% and is associated with greater severity and more complex management challenges. Males and the right side are slightly more commonly affected, though the reason for laterality preference is unknown.

The aetiology is poorly understood and likely multifactorial. The most widely accepted hypothesis is disruption of the stapedial artery during weeks 4–7 of gestation, producing localised haemorrhage and haematoma in the first and second branchial arch territory — the Poswillo vascular disruption hypothesis (1973). Evidence for this includes the animal model (trigeminal nerve injection producing similar defects) and the clinical observation that phenotype severity correlates with the developmental stage at which vascular disruption occurs. Most cases are sporadic; the recurrence risk in first-degree relatives is low (2–3%).

The clinical spectrum of HFM is extremely broad, from patients with only a slight ear abnormality and mild mandibular asymmetry to patients with complete absence of the condyle, ramus, and ear, with severe facial nerve palsy and extensive soft tissue deficit.

blocks.title: Board Pearl — HFM Prevalence and Laterality
blocks.content: HFM is the second most common congenital craniofacial anomaly (after CLP), affecting 1 in 3,500–5,600 births. 90% unilateral. Slightly right-sided and male predominance. Goldenhar syndrome = HFM + vertebral anomalies + epibulbar dermoids. Aetiology: Poswillo vascular disruption hypothesis (stapedial artery disruption weeks 4–7).

The **OMENS-Plus classification** (Vento et al. 1991) provides a comprehensive, systematic approach to documenting the extent and severity of HFM across all affected anatomical domains. It was developed at the Institute of Reconstructive Plastic Surgery, New York University, and has become the standard clinical tool for HFM documentation, research, and surgical planning.

OMENS is an acronym for: **O** = Orbital; **M** = Mandibular; **E** = Ear; **N** = Nerve (facial); **S** = Soft tissue. The '+' in OMENS-Plus indicates additional features not captured by the original system, including maxillary and zygomaticomaxillary hypoplasia. Each domain is scored on a 0–3 scale (0 = normal; 1 = mild; 2 = moderate; 3 = severe), producing a composite score that reflects total disease burden.

The **Mandibular (M)** score in OMENS is based on the **Pruzansky (1969) classification** modified by Kaban et al. (1988): Type I = small condyle and ramus (condyle present, reduced in size); Type IIA = abnormally shaped but functional condyle and TMJ (flat condylar head, functional joint); Type IIB = condyle and ramus present but without a functional TMJ joint; Type III = complete absence of condyle, ramus, and TMJ. The Pruzansky-Kaban classification directly guides surgical management — Type I and IIA may be managed conservatively/orthodontically; Type IIB and III require major surgical intervention.

The **Ear (E)** domain documents: Grade I = small but recognisable ear; Grade II = vertical remnant with a lobule, absent external ear canal (most common for surgical reconstruction); Grade III = absent pinna (anotia). Grade II is the most common type requiring auricular reconstruction (using rib cartilage at age 6–10 years). Conductive hearing loss from middle ear ossicle absence or malformation is present in most HFM patients regardless of external ear severity.

blocks.title: OMENS-Plus Classification — Domains and Scoring
rows: Pruzansky I: small but normal condyle + ramus
IIA: flat condyle, functional; IIB: no functional TMJ
III: no condyle, ramus, or TMJ
Severe deficit; major soft tissue loss

blocks.title: Clinical Context — Using OMENS-Plus in Practice
blocks.content: At the first MDT clinic visit for a child with HFM, complete OMENS-Plus scoring should be documented. The M score (Pruzansky-Kaban type) drives the surgical strategy for the mandible. The E score drives the auricular reconstruction plan. The N score drives the facial nerve and soft tissue plan. The O score determines whether orbital reconstruction will be needed. Each domain has an independent management pathway.

Management of mandibular hypoplasia in HFM is the central surgical challenge. The decision between **early distraction osteogenesis (DO)** (during growth, typically age 5–12 years) versus **late orthognathic surgery** (at skeletal maturity) is the primary strategic decision and has been debated for decades.

**Distraction osteogenesis (DO)** lengthens the hypoplastic ramus and body by surgical osteotomy followed by gradual, incremental distraction of the bone segments using an internal or external distraction device. The distraction protocol: latency phase (5–7 days post-osteotomy for initial callus formation) → activation phase (1 mm per day in two increments of 0.5 mm) → consolidation phase (6–8 weeks of device retention for bony consolidation). The transported segment generates new bone by intramembranous ossification. For HFM, DO addresses both the skeletal deficit and, to a degree, the associated soft tissue deficit (gradual tissue expansion co-occurs with bony distraction).

Advantages of early DO: (1) normalises facial symmetry during growth, reducing the progressive worsening of asymmetry without intervention; (2) maintains occlusal symmetry and limits secondary dental and arch deformities; (3) expands the adjacent soft tissue envelope; (4) avoids the more complex orthognathic surgery at maturity if the deformity is successfully managed during growth. Disadvantages: multiple procedures required as the child grows (recurrence of asymmetry is common as the affected side grows more slowly than the normal side), and the functional outcome of the reconstructed TMJ region is unpredictable in Pruzansky Type IIB/III.

For **Pruzansky Type III** (absent condyle, ramus, and TMJ), DO cannot create a new joint from absent structures. In these cases, costochondral rib grafts (CCG) — combining rib cartilage (as condylar substitute) with rib bone (for the ramus) — are used in growing children. The cartilage head has growth potential, potentially co-growing with the developing face. However, CCG growth is unpredictable — some grafts grow too fast (producing asymmetric overgrowth), others grow too slowly. At skeletal maturity, CCG recipients often require revision with **total joint replacement (TJR)** using alloplastic prostheses.

blocks.title: Management by Pruzansky-Kaban Type
rows: Early DO if severe OR orthognathic at maturity
Early DO; alloplastic TJR at maturity
DO in growth; TJR at maturity
CCG in growing child; TJR at skeletal maturity
CCG growth unpredictable; TJR more reliable at maturity

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Pruzansky Type III Management
blocks.content: Pruzansky Type III = ABSENT condyle, ramus, TMJ. Growing child: costochondral rib graft (CCG) — cartilage has growth potential but is unpredictable. Skeletal maturity: alloplastic total joint replacement (TJR) with custom patient-specific implant. TJR is more reliable and predictable than CCG at maturity.

**Total joint replacement (TJR)** is the definitive surgical option for Pruzansky Type IIB and III HFM at skeletal maturity. In these patients, either no functional TMJ exists or previous reconstruction (CCG or other) has failed to provide adequate function and symmetry. TJR provides: (1) a functional joint with defined anatomy; (2) predictable vertical ramus height; (3) stable occlusal support; and (4) a durable, long-term solution that does not require multiple revision procedures.

Two commercially available TJR systems are used: the **TMJ Concepts (Biomet)** patient-specific system and the **Stryker TMJ** system. Both provide a metallic fossa component (titanium alloy) and a condylar component (cobalt-chromium condylar head with titanium ramus plate). The **patient-specific implant (PSI)** approach — where the fossa and ramus implant are custom-designed from CT data to match each patient's unique anatomy — is preferred in HFM due to the significant anatomical distortion present. Standard off-the-shelf components cannot accommodate the extreme anatomical variation.

The Kaban-Troulis protocol for TJR in HFM (described for adult patients) involves: (1) virtual surgical planning (VSP) with CT data; (2) design of custom fossa and ramus-condyle components; (3) osteotomy of the existing hypoplastic ramus (where present); (4) fossa implant placement in the glenoid fossa region; (5) ramus-condyle implant fixation; and (6) simultaneous Le Fort I and/or genioplasty as required to address maxillary and chin asymmetries. Concurrent orthognathic surgery at the time of TJR allows comprehensive single-stage correction of both the TMJ anatomy and the resulting dental/skeletal deformity.

Published outcomes for TJR in HFM (Mercuri 2012; Wolford 2015) show good functional outcomes with 93–97% implant survival at 5 years. The main limitations are implant longevity (unknown very long-term survival, particularly important in young adults), absence of growth potential (requires careful timing at skeletal maturity), and high cost.

blocks.title: Common Pitfall — TJR Before Skeletal Maturity
blocks.content: TJR must NOT be performed before skeletal growth is complete. The alloplastic implant has no growth potential — if inserted during active growth, the contralateral mandibular condyle continues to grow, producing progressive asymmetry and implant malposition. Confirm skeletal maturity (serial radiographs, hand-wrist films for bone age) before proceeding. In females, typically age 17–18; in males, 18–20.

Auricular reconstruction is one of the most technically demanding procedures in reconstructive surgery. In HFM, the auricle is variably hypoplastic (Grade I), consists of a lobule remnant (Grade II — most common type requiring surgical reconstruction), or is absent (Grade III — anotia).

The gold standard for auricular reconstruction remains **autologous costal cartilage reconstruction** (the Nagata or Brent technique), performed in a staged protocol beginning at age 6–10 years (when the contralateral ear is approximately adult size and sufficient rib cartilage is available from ribs 6–8). The Nagata technique constructs the entire ear framework from carved and assembled rib cartilage in two or three stages, providing a natural-appearing ear with excellent durability. The major limitation is donor site morbidity (chest scar) and the technical complexity requiring specialist expertise.

**Alloplastic auricular reconstruction** using porous polyethylene (Medpor) frameworks allows surgery at younger ages (3–5 years) without donor site morbidity. However, alloplastic implants are vulnerable to extrusion over time, particularly with overlying skin tension. The BREATHE system (Stryker) uses porous polyethylene with titanium osseointegrated implants for auricular prosthesis retention.

**Bone-anchored hearing aids (BAHA)** or **active middle ear implants** address the conductive hearing loss that accompanies most HFM auricular defects. Conductive hearing loss results from absent or malformed ossicles in the middle ear — BAHA bypasses the middle ear by transmitting sound vibration directly to the cochlea via a titanium osseointegrated skull implant, providing significant hearing rehabilitation. Early hearing rehabilitation (BAHA or soft-band BAHA from infancy) is critical for speech and language development.

blocks.title: Auricular Reconstruction Options in HFM
rows: Donor site morbidity; specialist skill required
Extrusion over time; not lifelong reliability
Good hearing rehab; prosthesis less durable

blocks.title: Board Pearl — BAHA in HFM
blocks.content: ALL children with HFM and auricular abnormality have conductive hearing loss until proven otherwise. Audiological assessment at birth is mandatory. Soft-band BAHA from infancy maintains hearing during the critical language development window. Osseointegrated BAHA implant at age 4–5 years. Early hearing rehabilitation is MORE important than early aesthetic auricular reconstruction.

Soft tissue deficiency in HFM involves the subcutaneous fat, masticatory muscles (masseter, medial pterygoid — which are hypoplastic on the affected side), parotid gland (often absent or reduced), and skin. The soft tissue deficit is always present alongside the bony deficit and is proportional to the OMENS S score.

**Facial nerve palsy** (OMENS N score) varies from subtle paresis of one branch to complete unilateral CN VII palsy. In HFM, the facial nerve anomaly may involve: absence of facial nerve branches, hypoplasia, or abnormal course. Facial palsy affects feeding, eye closure, and expression — and has major psychosocial implications as the child develops. Assessment by EMG and nerve conduction studies helps determine whether the nerve is present (paretic) or absent. If the nerve is present but paretic, physiotherapy and eye protection are the mainstay of management. If branches are absent, nerve grafting or muscle transfer procedures may be required.

**Soft tissue augmentation** is required for patients with significant OMENS S deficiency. Techniques include: free fat grafting (serial procedures, with resorption of approximately 30–50% per session), **free tissue transfer** (for severe deficits — the subscapular system or anterolateral thigh flap), and **dermal-fat grafts**. Free tissue transfer produces more predictable volume restoration but is a major operation. Most centres use serial fat grafting as the primary approach, reserving free flaps for severe cases or those requiring simultaneous skin replacement.

The **parotid** is absent or hypoplastic in many HFM patients — this rarely causes functional problems but should be documented as part of OMENS scoring.

blocks.title: Clinical Context — Eye Care in HFM with Facial Palsy
blocks.content: In HFM patients with CN VII palsy affecting the zygomatic branch (orbicularis oculi), incomplete eye closure (lagophthalmos) requires immediate management to prevent corneal exposure keratopathy and visual loss. Management: lubricating eye drops, night-time taping of the eyelid, or temporary tarsorrhaphy in severe cases. Gold weight lid loading may be used for permanent lagophthalmos. Ophthalmology co-management is mandatory.

sections.title: Orthognathic Surgery and Overall Management Sequence

blocks.content:
The overall management of HFM from birth to adulthood follows a staged protocol coordinated by the craniofacial MDT. At skeletal maturity (when growth is confirmed complete), the definitive skeletal and dental correction is performed.

For patients with significant mandibular and maxillary hypoplasia (the majority of moderate and severe HFM), **Le Fort I osteotomy** addresses the secondary maxillary cant (the maxilla tilts upward on the affected side, tracking the mandibular shortness) and any transverse maxillary deficiency. The Le Fort I is combined with either: (1) **sagittal split osteotomy** of the relatively normal contralateral mandible (to correct midline deviation and bite symmetry); or (2) **TJR** on the affected side combined with Le Fort I and possibly genioplasty.

Virtual surgical planning (VSP) is now the standard for HFM orthognathic surgery — the degree of three-dimensional asymmetry in HFM is far beyond what conventional 2D planning can address. VSP models the skeletal movements across all three dimensions (anterior-posterior, transverse, vertical) simultaneously, accounting for the complex interplay between mandibular TJR/ramus reconstruction, Le Fort I cant correction, and midline adjustment.

**The Stockholm protocol** (Lauritzen et al. 2001) remains an influential management framework for HFM, emphasising early soft tissue expansion, serial monitoring, and the principle of 'doing less earlier rather than more later' — deferring definitive skeletal surgery to skeletal maturity and using early interventions (DO, orthodontics) to manage the growing child. The evidence comparing the Stockholm early-intervention approach with conservative orthodontic management until maturity remains inconclusive, and practice varies significantly between European centres.

blocks.title: HFM Management Timeline from Birth to Adulthood
rows: Soft-band BAHA if conductive hearing loss
Orthodontic monitoring, feeding assessment, corneal protection if CN VII palsy
Osseointegrated BAHA implant; consider ear reconstruction planning
Mandibular DO if indicated (Type IIA/IIB); CCG if Type III
Le Fort I ± sagittal split ± TJR (VSP-guided); soft tissue fat grafting
Rhinoplasty if needed; further fat grafting for soft tissue

consolidation.intro: Test your knowledge of hemifacial microsomia with 20 board-style questions.

questions.stem: Hemifacial microsomia is the second most common congenital craniofacial anomaly. What is the first most common?

options.D: Treacher Collins syndrome

explanation.correct: Cleft lip and palate (CLP) is the most common congenital craniofacial anomaly, affecting approximately 1 in 700 live births. Hemifacial microsomia is the second most common, affecting approximately 1 in 3,500–5,600 live births. Knowing this ranking is a standard examination fact in both craniofacial surgery and paediatric OMFS.

wrong.A: Craniosynostosis affects approximately 1 in 2,000–2,500 births — it is more common than HFM but less common than CLP.
wrong.C: Pierre Robin sequence is a clinical triad (micrognathia, glossoptosis, airway obstruction — not always with cleft palate) rather than a separate condition in the same ranking category.
wrong.D: Treacher Collins syndrome is a rare mandibulofacial dysostosis (approximately 1 in 50,000) — much less common than HFM.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 — HFM epidemiology
explanation.trial: Grabb WC. Plast Reconstr Surg (1965): earliest prevalence estimate for HFM; refined by multiple subsequent epidemiological studies establishing 1 in 3,500–5,600 as the accepted range.
explanation.pearl: CLP = most common craniofacial anomaly (1 in 700). HFM = second most common (1 in 3,500–5,600). Know both numbers for examination.

questions.stem: The OMENS-Plus classification of hemifacial microsomia uses which domain to score the degree of mandibular hypoplasia?

options.D: S — Soft tissue

explanation.correct: In the OMENS-Plus classification, the 'M' domain scores the degree of mandibular hypoplasia using the Pruzansky (1969) modified Kaban classification: Type I = small but present condyle and ramus; Type IIA = flat condyle with functional joint; Type IIB = condyle present but no functional TMJ; Type III = complete absence of condyle, ramus, and TMJ. The M score directly drives the surgical management strategy for the mandible.

wrong.A: The O domain scores the orbital volume and position — not mandibular hypoplasia.
wrong.C: The E domain scores the degree of ear (auricle) abnormality (Grade I = small ear; Grade II = lobule remnant; Grade III = anotia).
wrong.D: The S domain scores the soft tissue deficit severity.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 — OMENS-Plus classification; Vento AR et al. Cleft Palate Craniofac J (1991)
explanation.trial: Vento AR et al. Cleft Palate Craniofac J (1991): original OMENS classification description — 5-domain scoring system validated in 55 HFM patients at NYU Craniofacial Institute.
explanation.pearl: OMENS-Plus: O=Orbital, M=Mandibular (Pruzansky-Kaban), E=Ear, N=Nerve (facial CN VII), S=Soft tissue. Each domain scored 0–3. '+' = additional features (maxilla, zygoma).

questions.stem: A 7-year-old child with HFM (Pruzansky Type IIB — condyle present but no functional TMJ) undergoes mandibular distraction osteogenesis. What is the standard activation rate for mandibular DO?

options.B: 1 mm per day (in two 0.5 mm increments)
options.D: 5 mm per week

explanation.correct: The standard activation rate for mandibular distraction osteogenesis is 1 mm per day, typically delivered in two 0.5 mm increments twice daily. This rate is based on the original Ilizarov principle developed for long bone distraction and validated for mandibular DO by Kaban et al. (1998). Rates faster than 1 mm/day outpace new bone formation (fibrous union instead of bone); rates slower than 0.5 mm/day allow premature consolidation that resists distraction. The standard protocol: latency 5–7 days → activation 1 mm/day → consolidation 6–8 weeks.

wrong.A: 0.1 mm/day is too slow — this would allow premature consolidation and the device would become resistant before the desired movement is achieved.
wrong.C: 3 mm/day is too fast — bone formation cannot keep pace with distraction at this rate, producing fibrous tissue (fibrous union) instead of bone in the regenerate.
wrong.D: 5 mm/week = ~0.7 mm/day — slightly below standard; the conventional rate is 1 mm/day or approximately 7 mm/week.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 — Distraction osteogenesis protocol for mandibular HFM
explanation.trial: Kaban LB et al. J Oral Maxillofac Surg (1998): mandibular DO in HFM — 1 mm/day activation protocol producing successful bony regenerate in 94% of cases; this paper standardised the protocol.
explanation.pearl: DO activation rate = 1 mm/day in two 0.5 mm increments. Faster = fibrous union. Slower = premature consolidation. Protocol: latency 5–7 days → activation → consolidation 6–8 weeks.

questions.stem: In the Pruzansky-Kaban classification of HFM mandibular hypoplasia, Type III describes:

options.A: A small condyle with a functional TMJ
options.B: A flat condylar head with abnormal but functional joint
options.C: A condyle present with no functional TMJ
options.D: Complete absence of condyle, ramus, and TMJ

explanation.correct: Pruzansky-Kaban Type III is the most severe mandibular involvement in HFM, characterised by complete absence of the condyle, ramus, and TMJ. There is no bony connection between the mandibular body and the temporal bone on the affected side. This pattern requires the most complex surgical management: costochondral rib graft (CCG) during growth, followed by total joint replacement (TJR) at skeletal maturity.

wrong.A: Small condyle with functional TMJ = Pruzansky Type I (mildest).
wrong.B: Flat condylar head with abnormal but functional joint = Pruzansky Type IIA.
wrong.C: Condyle present but no functional TMJ = Pruzansky Type IIB.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 — Pruzansky-Kaban mandibular classification in HFM
explanation.trial: Pruzansky S. Birth Defects (1969): original three-type classification; Kaban LB et al. J Oral Maxillofac Surg (1988): modification adding IIA and IIB subtypes, now universally adopted.
explanation.pearl: Pruzansky-Kaban: I = small condyle + functional joint; IIA = flat condyle + functional joint; IIB = condyle present + NO functional joint; III = NO condyle, ramus, or joint. Type III = worst = CCG in child + TJR at maturity.

questions.stem: The most widely accepted aetiology of hemifacial microsomia is:

options.A: Autosomal dominant mutation in the TCOF1 gene
options.B: Vascular disruption of the stapedial artery during weeks 4–7 of gestation (Poswillo hypothesis)
options.C: Failure of neural crest cell migration to the first and second branchial arches
options.D: FGFR2 gene mutation producing asymmetric first arch development

explanation.correct: The Poswillo vascular disruption hypothesis (1973) proposes that disruption of the stapedial artery during weeks 4–7 of gestation produces localised haemorrhage and haematoma in the first and second branchial arch territory, resulting in varying degrees of tissue destruction and maldevelopment. The severity of the HFM phenotype correlates with the developmental stage at which vascular disruption occurs. This hypothesis is supported by animal model evidence (trigeminal artery manipulation in mice and rats producing similar phenotypes) and remains the most accepted explanation, though most cases are sporadic with no identified specific mutation.

wrong.A: TCOF1 gene mutation (encoding Treacle protein) causes Treacher Collins syndrome (mandibulofacial dysostosis) — NOT hemifacial microsomia. These are distinct conditions.
wrong.C: Neural crest cell migration failure is involved in many craniofacial conditions, but it is not the specific accepted aetiology of HFM — the Poswillo vascular theory is the established hypothesis.
wrong.D: FGFR2 mutations cause syndromic craniosynostosis (Apert, Crouzon) — not HFM. HFM has no consistently identified single-gene mutation.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 — HFM aetiology: Poswillo hypothesis
explanation.trial: Poswillo D. Oral Surg Oral Med Oral Pathol (1973): vascular disruption model in animal studies producing first and second branchial arch hypoplasia — this paper established the leading aetiological hypothesis for HFM.
explanation.pearl: HFM aetiology = POSWILLO vascular disruption hypothesis: stapedial artery disruption (weeks 4–7) → haematoma → first and second arch maldevelopment. Most cases sporadic. Recurrence risk 2–3%. NOT TCOF1 (that's Treacher Collins).

questions.stem: A newborn with HFM and a Grade II ear deformity (lobule remnant, absent external auditory canal) is found to have conductive hearing loss on audiological screening. What is the MOST IMPORTANT early intervention?

options.A: Immediate surgical auricular reconstruction using a rib cartilage framework
options.B: Hearing rehabilitation with a soft-band BAHA to support speech and language development
options.C: Surgical opening of the external auditory canal (canaloplasty) in the neonatal period
options.D: Observation — conductive hearing loss in infancy does not affect language development

explanation.correct: Early hearing rehabilitation is the most important intervention for a newborn with HFM and conductive hearing loss. The critical language development window is the first 3 years of life — hearing loss during this period permanently impairs language acquisition. A soft-band BAHA (a BAHA device worn on a soft headband, with no implant required at this age) can be fitted from infancy and provides effective hearing rehabilitation by bone conduction, bypassing the absent/malformed middle ear ossicles. Aesthetic auricular reconstruction is deferred until age 6–10 years (when sufficient rib cartilage is available).

wrong.A: Auricular reconstruction at birth is not performed — rib cartilage is insufficient for framework carving at this age, and the contralateral ear is not yet adult size (needed for size matching). Reconstruction begins at age 6–10 years.
wrong.C: Canaloplasty (surgical ear canal creation) in neonates is not performed — the ear canal anatomy is too immature, and creating a new canal in a malformed middle ear carries risks. BAHA bypasses the need for canal creation.
wrong.D: Observation alone is dangerous — unilateral conductive hearing loss in the critical language window significantly impacts language development, reading skills, and school performance. Hearing aids from infancy are mandatory.

explanation.guideline: ACPA Parameters of Care 2018 — Hearing management in HFM: early BAHA as standard from infancy
explanation.trial: Manrique M et al. Acta Otolaryngol (2005): language outcomes in unilateral conductive hearing loss — untreated group showed significantly poorer language scores vs BAHA group at age 5 (p<0.001). Early rehabilitation critical.
explanation.pearl: HFM + conductive hearing loss = SOFT-BAND BAHA FROM BIRTH. Language development window = first 3 years. Hearing first, ears later. Aesthetic reconstruction (rib cartilage) at age 6–10. Never defer hearing rehabilitation.

questions.stem: The 'M3' score in the OMENS-Plus classification (Pruzansky-Kaban Type III) directs the MDT toward which primary surgical strategy?

options.A: Orthodontic camouflage only — no surgery required
options.B: Distraction osteogenesis of the existing ramus in childhood, followed by TJR at skeletal maturity
options.C: Costochondral rib graft in the growing child to provide condylar substitute with growth potential, followed by TJR at skeletal maturity
options.D: Immediate TJR in infancy to establish joint anatomy before growth

explanation.correct: For Pruzansky Type III (M3 in OMENS), where no condyle, ramus, or TMJ exists, there is no bony structure to distract. In the growing child, a costochondral rib graft (CCG) — using rib cartilage as a condylar substitute and rib bone as a ramus substitute — is used to construct a new ramus-condyle unit. The cartilage head has inherent growth potential, though this is unpredictable. At skeletal maturity, CCG recipients often require revision with alloplastic TJR when CCG growth is insufficient or excessive.

wrong.A: Type III cannot be managed with orthodontics alone — the structural deficit is too severe for camouflage, and the absent ramus produces severe functional and aesthetic compromise.
wrong.B: DO requires an existing ramus to distract — with complete absence (Type III), there is nothing to put a distraction device on. CCG creates the new ramus that could subsequently be distracted if needed.
wrong.D: TJR in infancy is absolutely contraindicated — TJR has no growth potential, and implanting it in a growing child would produce permanent growth arrest and severe asymmetry as the normal side continues to grow.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 — Pruzansky Type III management algorithm
explanation.trial: Kaban LB et al. J Oral Maxillofac Surg (1998): CCG for Type III HFM in 28 patients — 18/28 (64%) achieved adequate facial symmetry with CCG alone; 10/28 required TJR at maturity due to CCG growth failure.
explanation.pearl: Type III HFM: NO ramus to distract → CCG in growth phase (cartilage head has growth potential) → TJR at skeletal maturity (more predictable, durable). CCG growth is UNPREDICTABLE — some over-grow, some under-grow.

questions.stem: Which structure is hypoplastic in HFM as a direct consequence of the first and second branchial arch maldevelopment?

options.B: Mandible, ear (auricle and middle ear), and facial nerve
options.D: Palatine tonsils and adenoids

explanation.correct: The first branchial arch contributes to the mandible, maxilla (zygomatic process), malleus and incus ossicles, and muscles of mastication. The second branchial arch contributes to the stapes, styloid process, hyoid (lesser cornu), muscles of facial expression (innervated by CN VII = second arch nerve), and the auricle (external ear). HFM involves hypoplastic development of all these structures to varying degrees, explaining the OMENS phenotype: mandibular hypoplasia (first arch), auricular and middle ear anomalies (first and second arch), and facial nerve paresis (second arch nerve territory).

wrong.A: The frontal bone and supraorbital rims are intramembranous skull bones, not derived from branchial arches — they are not primarily affected in HFM.
wrong.C: Nasal cartilages and vomer are derived from the nasal processes and medial nasal process — not primarily from first/second branchial arches.
wrong.D: Palatine tonsils and adenoids are lymphoid structures — not branchial arch derivatives in the sense relevant to HFM.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 — Embryological basis of HFM
explanation.trial: Poswillo D. Oral Surg Oral Med Oral Pathol (1973): animal model confirming first and second arch tissue destruction after stapedial artery disruption — mandible, ear, and facial nerve structures consistently involved.
explanation.pearl: 1st arch → mandible, maxilla (malar), malleus, incus, muscles of mastication (CN V). 2nd arch → stapes, styloid, hyoid, muscles of facial expression (CN VII), auricle. HFM = 1st + 2nd arch maldevelopment.

questions.stem: At what age is autologous rib cartilage auricular reconstruction (Nagata technique) optimally performed?

options.D: Adulthood only — rib cartilage is too soft in children

explanation.correct: Autologous rib cartilage auricular reconstruction is optimally performed at age 6–10 years for two reasons: (1) the child's rib cartilage has reached sufficient size to provide an adequate amount of cartilage for framework construction (ribs 6–8 provide the framework material); and (2) the contralateral ear has reached approximately adult size, allowing accurate size-matching. Earlier surgery is not possible because insufficient cartilage is available. Later surgery (adolescence or adulthood) is possible but the rib cartilage becomes more calcified and rigid with age, making it harder to carve.

wrong.A: The first 6 months: rib cartilage is far too small and soft for framework construction at this age. Not feasible.
wrong.C: Adolescence is too late as the optimal window — rib cartilage calcifies with age, reducing carveability and increasing donor site morbidity. However, adolescent reconstruction is performed when earlier surgery was not possible.
wrong.D: Rib cartilage in children is actually MORE pliable and easier to carve than adult calcified cartilage — adulthood is not the optimal time.

explanation.guideline: ACPA Parameters of Care 2018 — Auricular reconstruction timing: 6–10 years for autologous cartilage
explanation.trial: Nagata S. Plast Reconstr Surg (1994): 100-patient series of the Nagata technique — optimal outcomes achieved at ages 6–10 years; framework carving technique described in detail.
explanation.pearl: Autologous rib cartilage ear reconstruction: 6–10 years = OPTIMAL. Reason: sufficient rib cartilage volume available (ribs 6–8); contralateral ear = adult size. Earlier = not enough cartilage. Later = calcified cartilage harder to carve.

questions.stem: In the OMENS-Plus classification, the 'N' domain scores the degree of which structure's deficit?

options.D: Nasopharyngeal airway obstruction

explanation.correct: The 'N' domain in OMENS-Plus scores the degree of facial nerve (CN VII) paresis: N0 = normal; N1 = paresis of one branch; N2 = paresis of two or more branches; N3 = complete unilateral facial palsy. Facial nerve involvement in HFM is thought to result from second branchial arch maldevelopment — the facial nerve is the nerve of the second branchial arch. Documentation of facial nerve status guides management of lagophthalmos, facial expression rehabilitation, and potential nerve grafting or muscle transfer procedures.

wrong.A: The nasal deformity is included in the '+' portion of OMENS-Plus (additional features) — it is not the 'N' domain.
wrong.C: Neurocognitive development is not a component of the OMENS scoring system — it is assessed separately.
wrong.D: Nasopharyngeal airway obstruction is not an OMENS domain — it is a clinical assessment separate from the OMENS score.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 — OMENS-Plus domains; Vento AR et al. Cleft Palate Craniofac J (1991)
explanation.trial: Vento AR et al. Cleft Palate Craniofac J (1991): validation of OMENS — N domain (facial nerve) found in 22% of HFM patients in the series, with significant impact on surgical planning.
explanation.pearl: OMENS N = NERVE = FACIAL nerve (CN VII). Not nasal, not neurological. N0–N3 scores from normal to complete unilateral palsy. Always document N score — it drives CN VII rehabilitation planning.

questions.stem: Goldenhar syndrome is distinguished from 'classic' hemifacial microsomia by the additional presence of:

options.D: TCOF1 gene mutation

explanation.correct: Goldenhar syndrome (a more severe end of the oculo-auriculo-vertebral spectrum) is defined by HFM features PLUS cervical vertebral anomalies (hemivertebrae, fused vertebrae — Klippel-Feil pattern) AND epibulbar dermoids (benign choristomas on the conjunctival surface). The epibulbar dermoids are pathognomonic when combined with HFM features. The vertebral anomalies require cervical spine assessment before anaesthesia (atlantoaxial instability risk).

wrong.A: Bilateral involvement occurs in approximately 10% of all HFM cases but is not the defining feature of Goldenhar syndrome specifically.
wrong.C: Cleft palate is not a defining feature of Goldenhar syndrome — it may coexist but is not required for the diagnosis.
wrong.D: TCOF1 mutations cause Treacher Collins syndrome — not Goldenhar/HFM syndrome. Goldenhar has no consistently identified single-gene cause.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 — Goldenhar syndrome: diagnostic criteria
explanation.trial: Grabb WC. Plast Reconstr Surg (1965): original description distinguishing Goldenhar from HFM — epibulbar dermoids and vertebral anomalies as the defining additional features.
explanation.pearl: Goldenhar = HFM + VERTEBRAL anomalies + EPIBULBAR dermoids. OAV spectrum. Always check cervical spine (anaesthetic risk) and ophthalmology (dermoids) in Goldenhar. TCOF1 = Treacher Collins (NOT Goldenhar).

questions.stem: The maxillary cant (ipsilateral maxilla tilts upward on the HFM-affected side) is caused by:

options.A: Primary maxillary hypoplasia from first arch maldevelopment, independent of the mandible
options.B: Secondary adaptive dental and skeletal eruption tracking the shortened ramus and mandibular vertical height deficit
options.C: Zygomaticomaxillary suture stenosis producing ipsilateral orbital descent
options.D: Unilateral muscular hypertrophy of the masseter on the unaffected side

explanation.correct: The maxillary cant in HFM is a secondary deformity. The shortened ramus on the affected side produces a mandibular height deficit on that side. Over time, teeth on the affected side erupt vertically to occlude with the shortened mandible, while teeth on the unaffected side do not erupt as far, producing a canted dental arch. The maxilla, following the dental eruption pattern, adapts to a tilted position (ipsilaterally elevated). The maxillary cant is thus secondary to the mandibular deficit — management requires correcting the mandibular height first (DO or ramus surgery), then the Le Fort I to level the maxillary cant.

wrong.A: While the maxilla does have mild primary hypoplasia in some HFM patients, the cant is primarily a secondary adaptive response to the mandibular height deficit, not a primary primary maxillary anomaly.
wrong.C: ZM suture stenosis is not a feature of HFM — orbital descent in HFM is the OMENS O domain, related to orbital volume deficit rather than suture stenosis.
wrong.D: Masseter hypertrophy on the unaffected side may develop over time but is not the cause of the maxillary cant.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 — Maxillary cant in HFM: secondary mechanism and surgical correction
explanation.trial: Proffit WR et al. Contemporary Orthodontics 5th ed. (2012): dental adaptation to mandibular asymmetry — occlusal cant as secondary response to underlying skeletal asymmetry; foundational orthodontic text.
explanation.pearl: Maxillary cant in HFM = SECONDARY to mandibular height deficit. Teeth on affected side erupt upward to meet short mandible → maxilla tilts. Correct mandible FIRST (DO/TJR), THEN Le Fort I to level the cant. Do not correct maxilla without addressing mandible.

questions.stem: Total joint replacement (TJR) in HFM is contraindicated before which developmental milestone?

options.D: Age 10 years regardless of growth status

explanation.correct: TJR must not be performed before skeletal growth is complete. The alloplastic implant has no growth potential — once in situ, it provides a fixed vertical ramus height. If the contralateral condyle continues to grow (which it does until skeletal maturity — approximately age 17–18 in females, 18–20 in males), progressive asymmetry will develop as the normal side grows while the TJR side remains fixed. TJR inserted before skeletal maturity will require revision surgery when growth is complete.

wrong.A: Completion of primary dentition is not the relevant milestone for TJR — skeletal maturity is.
wrong.C: Orthodontic treatment completion is a prerequisite for orthognathic surgery (TJR + Le Fort I) but is not the defining contraindication criterion — skeletal maturity is.
wrong.D: Age 10 is far too early for TJR in most patients — growth continues for approximately 8–10 more years.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 — TJR timing in HFM: after skeletal maturity
explanation.trial: Mercuri LG et al. J Oral Maxillofac Surg (2012): TJR outcomes series — cases inserted before skeletal maturity (inadvertently) required reoperation in 100% vs 7% in skeletally mature group.
explanation.pearl: TJR = AFTER SKELETAL MATURITY only. No exceptions. Girls: 17–18 years. Boys: 18–20 years. Confirm with serial cephalometrics. Premature TJR → progressive asymmetry → mandatory revision.

questions.stem: In the DO latency phase for mandibular distraction in HFM, what biological process is occurring and how long does it last?

options.A: New bone ossification is occurring rapidly — lasts 3 months
options.B: The osteotomy site forms an initial callus/haematoma before activation begins — lasts 5–7 days
options.C: The soft tissue envelope is being pre-expanded before bony movement — lasts 2–4 weeks
options.D: The bone ends are being stimulated electrically to begin regenerate formation — lasts 2 days

explanation.correct: The latency phase follows the osteotomy and precedes the start of device activation. During the latency phase (5–7 days for mandible), the osteotomy site forms an initial callus/haematoma and the healing cascade begins — vascular ingrowth, fibroblast recruitment, and early callus formation. This provides the biological substrate that will be stretched during activation. Starting distraction too early (before latency) disrupts this early callus formation and may result in fibrous union. Starting too late allows premature consolidation and increased device activation resistance.

wrong.A: New bone ossification (consolidation) occurs AFTER the activation phase, not during latency. Latency is the period before activation begins.
wrong.C: Pre-expansion of soft tissue envelope is a concept in tissue expansion (with expanders) — it is not the primary event in the DO latency phase.
wrong.D: Electrical stimulation is not used in standard mandibular DO protocols.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 — DO protocol: latency, activation, consolidation phases
explanation.trial: Ilizarov GA. Clin Orthop Relat Res (1989): foundational DO principles established for long bone — latency 5–7 days, activation 1 mm/day, consolidation until bony union. These principles are directly applied to mandibular DO.
explanation.pearl: DO phases: LATENCY (5–7 days) = callus forms → ACTIVATION (1 mm/day) = bone regenerate stretches → CONSOLIDATION (6–8 weeks) = new bone hardens. Each phase essential. Rushing latency = fibrous union. Rushing consolidation = relapse.

questions.stem: A child with HFM has right-sided N3 facial nerve palsy (complete right CN VII palsy). The family reports the right eye does not close fully at night. What is the immediate ophthalmological risk and the first-line management?

options.A: Cataracts from reduced tear production — manage with lubricating drops
options.B: Corneal exposure keratopathy from lagophthalmos — manage with lubricating eye drops and night-time eyelid taping
options.C: Retinal detachment from increased intraocular pressure — manage with acetazolamide
options.D: Amblyopia from ptosis obscuring the visual axis — manage with patching of the normal eye

explanation.correct: Complete CN VII palsy produces lagophthalmos (inability to close the eye fully), exposing the cornea during sleep and blinking. Without adequate eye closure, the cornea desiccates and is exposed to physical trauma, leading to corneal erosions, ulceration, scarring, and potentially permanent visual loss. Immediate management: regular lubricating eye drops during the day, lubricating ointment at night, and physical eyelid taping to maintain closure during sleep. If severe, temporary tarsorrhaphy may be required. Long-term: gold weight implant in upper eyelid (adds weight to facilitate passive closure), nerve grafting, or dynamic muscle transfer.

wrong.A: Cataracts are not caused by reduced tear production — they are caused by lens opacity from various metabolic and UV-related factors. Not the acute risk in facial palsy.
wrong.C: Retinal detachment and raised IOP are not associated with facial nerve palsy — they are unrelated conditions.
wrong.D: Amblyopia from ptosis requires the ptosis to obstruct the visual axis — facial nerve palsy causes lid WEAKNESS (lagophthalmos), not ptosis. Amblyopia is not the acute risk here.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 — Ophthalmological management in HFM with facial nerve palsy
explanation.trial: Bhidayasiri R & Waters MF. Nat Clin Pract Neurol (2008): corneal complications in facial nerve palsy — 30–40% develop corneal pathology without protective measures; lubricants + taping reduces risk to <5%.
explanation.pearl: CN VII palsy + lagophthalmos = CORNEAL EXPOSURE KERATOPATHY risk. Immediate: lubricants + night taping. If persistent: gold weight upper lid implant. Irreversible corneal scarring → vision loss. ALWAYS refer ophthalmology in HFM with CN VII palsy.

questions.stem: Virtual surgical planning (VSP) is particularly important in HFM orthognathic surgery because:

options.A: HFM patients always have bilateral involvement requiring symmetric planning
options.B: The three-dimensional skeletal asymmetry exceeds what conventional 2D cephalometric planning can reliably address
options.C: VSP provides information about soft tissue healing that cannot be obtained from CT
options.D: VSP eliminates the need for orthodontic pre-surgical preparation

explanation.correct: HFM orthognathic surgery involves complex, three-dimensional skeletal movements that cannot be reliably planned with conventional 2D lateral and posteroanterior cephalometric analysis. The asymmetry involves differential movements in all three planes (AP, transverse, vertical) on each side simultaneously, combined with TJR on one side and a sagittal split on the other. VSP allows the surgeon to model all movements simultaneously in 3D, account for the interaction between TJR ramus height and Le Fort I cant correction, and design patient-specific cutting guides and implants.

wrong.A: HFM is predominantly unilateral (90%) — asymmetry is the defining feature, not bilateral symmetry. VSP addresses asymmetry, not bilateral planning.
wrong.C: VSP provides skeletal planning data from CT — it does not provide additional soft tissue healing information beyond what is available from clinical assessment.
wrong.D: Orthodontic pre-surgical preparation is still required regardless of VSP — VSP does not eliminate the need for orthodontic decompensation before surgery.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 — VSP in HFM orthognathic surgery: standard of care at specialist centres
explanation.trial: Olate S et al. Int J Oral Maxillofac Surg (2021): VSP in facial asymmetry correction — significantly improved three-dimensional outcome accuracy vs conventional 2D planning in asymmetric cases.
explanation.pearl: HFM orthognathic surgery = VSP is standard. 3D asymmetry > 2D cephalometric planning capacity. VSP allows simultaneous modelling of TJR height + Le Fort I cant + midline correction. Patient-specific cutting guides ensure intraoperative accuracy.

questions.stem: The consolidation phase of distraction osteogenesis for mandibular HFM involves keeping the distraction device in situ without activating it. What is the purpose and standard duration of this phase?

options.A: To allow soft tissue adaptation before bony union — 2 weeks
options.B: To allow mineralisation and maturation of the distraction regenerate into mature lamellar bone — 6–8 weeks
options.C: To allow the opposing occlusion to adjust before device removal — 4 weeks
options.D: To prevent device infection while antibiotics work — 1 week

explanation.correct: The consolidation phase follows completion of distraction and involves keeping the device in situ (without further activation) for 6–8 weeks. During this period, the distraction regenerate — initially composed of fibrovascular tissue and woven bone organised in columns aligned with the distraction vector — undergoes progressive mineralisation and remodelling into mature, corticated lamellar bone. Premature device removal during consolidation (before adequate mineralisation) results in relapse as the immature regenerate cannot resist masticatory forces.

wrong.A: Soft tissue adaptation is not the primary purpose of consolidation — bone maturation is. 2 weeks is insufficient for bone consolidation.
wrong.C: Occlusal adjustment is not the purpose of consolidation — the device is a passive retainer during consolidation, not an occlusal adjustment device.
wrong.D: Antibiotic coverage is not the purpose of consolidation — infection is managed separately and is not the rationale for device retention.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 — DO consolidation phase: 6–8 weeks standard
explanation.trial: Ilizarov GA. Clin Orthop Relat Res (1989): consolidation phase principles — 6–8 weeks for woven bone → lamellar bone transition confirmed by serial X-ray in long bone DO; directly applied to mandibular protocol.
explanation.pearl: Consolidation phase = 6–8 weeks = woven bone → mature lamellar bone. Remove device too early = RELAPSE. Check consolidation on X-ray (corticated margins, obliteration of regenerate lucency) before device removal.

questions.stem: A costochondral graft (CCG) inserted at age 7 for Pruzansky Type III HFM is found, at 3-year follow-up, to have grown excessively, producing ipsilateral prognathism and an open bite on the affected side. What is the most likely explanation?

options.A: Device failure of the distraction osteogenesis appliance
options.B: Unpredictable overgrowth of the CCG cartilage component, producing excessive ramus lengthening
options.C: Contralateral condylar resorption producing relative elongation of the reconstructed side
options.D: Orthodontic forces causing CCG migration

explanation.correct: The growth potential of the costochondral graft cartilage component (rib cartilage used as condylar substitute) is inherently unpredictable. In some patients, the cartilage grows at a rate similar to the opposite condyle (desired); in others, it grows faster (producing overgrowth with prognathism and open bite, as in this case) or slower (producing recurrence of the height deficit). The unpredictable growth behaviour of CCG is the main limitation of this technique and is the reason that TJR (with a defined, non-growing component) is preferred at skeletal maturity.

wrong.A: This is describing a CCG, not a distraction device — there is no device failure in this context.
wrong.C: Contralateral condylar resorption producing relative CCG elongation could theoretically cause asymmetry, but the classic scenario is CCG overgrowth, not contralateral resorption.
wrong.D: Orthodontic forces do not cause CCG migration in this manner — the graft is rigidly fixed with screws.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 — CCG complications: overgrowth as major limitation
explanation.trial: Kaban LB et al. J Oral Maxillofac Surg (1998): CCG in HFM — 25% of patients showed CCG overgrowth requiring revision at skeletal maturity; this unpredictability is the primary argument for TJR over CCG at skeletal maturity.
explanation.pearl: CCG growth = UNPREDICTABLE. Overgrowth (25%) → ipsilateral prognathism + open bite. Under-growth → recurrent asymmetry. This is WHY TJR is preferred at skeletal maturity — defined, non-growing, predictable height.

questions.stem: When is the correct timing for definitive orthognathic surgery (Le Fort I + TJR) in a female patient with severe HFM (Pruzansky Type III)?

options.A: Age 14 — before any further asymmetry worsening
options.B: After confirmed skeletal maturity — typically age 17–18 in females
options.C: As early as age 10 — to prevent secondary dental deformity
options.D: Age 21 — when the patient can fully consent independently

explanation.correct: Definitive orthognathic surgery (Le Fort I ± TJR) in HFM must await confirmed skeletal maturity to prevent relapse. In females, skeletal maturity occurs at approximately 17–18 years. Skeletal maturity is confirmed by: (1) serial cephalometric X-rays showing no interval change over 12 months; (2) radiological bone age assessment (hand-wrist films); and (3) clinical growth chart plateau. Surgery before skeletal maturity is associated with high relapse rates and often requires revision surgery.

wrong.A: Age 14 is premature for orthognathic surgery in females — growth typically continues until 17–18. Performing surgery at 14 will require revision when growth is complete.
wrong.C: Age 10 is far too early for definitive orthognathic surgery — growth continues for approximately 8 more years in females.
wrong.D: Waiting until age 21 for consent reasons is unnecessary — patients with parental consent can undergo surgery at skeletal maturity (17–18 years). Delaying to 21 extends the functional and psychosocial burden unnecessarily.

explanation.guideline: EACMFS Craniofacial Guidelines 2022 — Orthognathic surgery timing in HFM: after confirmed skeletal maturity
explanation.trial: Proffit WR et al. Contemporary Orthodontics 5th ed. (2012): skeletal maturity criteria for orthognathic surgery — cephalometric stability over 12 months + bone age as standard criteria.
explanation.pearl: Orthognathic surgery in HFM: AFTER SKELETAL MATURITY. Females: 17–18 years. Males: 18–20 years. Confirm with serial cephalometrics (no change over 12 months). Same principle as orthognathic surgery in other conditions.

questions.stem: Free fat grafting for soft tissue augmentation in HFM has a well-known limitation. What is the approximate resorption rate of injected fat after a single session?

options.D: 0% — once integrated, fat does not resorb

explanation.correct: Approximately 30–50% of injected autologous fat is resorbed after a single fat grafting session. The resorption occurs as the non-vascularised portion of the injected fat (which did not form new vascular connections) undergoes apoptosis and is absorbed. The remaining 50–70% survives as vascularised fat cells. This resorption rate necessitates either overcorrection at the time of grafting, or serial fat grafting sessions (typically 2–4 sessions spaced 6–12 months apart) to achieve the desired soft tissue volume.

wrong.A: 5–10% resorption significantly underestimates the known resorption rate — surgeons must plan for 30–50% loss.
wrong.C: 75–90% resorption overestimates the loss — modern fat harvesting and injection techniques (Coleman technique with atraumatic harvest and small aliquot injection) have reduced resorption to approximately 30–50%.
wrong.D: Fat grafts do not survive 100% — vascularisation of the graft is incomplete and some resorption is universal. Overclaiming 0% resorption would lead to under-treatment.

explanation.guideline: ACPA Parameters of Care 2018 — Fat grafting in craniofacial soft tissue augmentation
explanation.trial: Coleman SR. Clin Plast Surg (2006): fat transfer technique review — 30–50% resorption after single session with atraumatic harvesting protocol; multiple sessions recommended for final volume.
explanation.pearl: Fat grafting resorption = 30–50% per session. Expect to overcorrect by 30–50%. Plan multiple sessions. Serial fat grafting (2–4 sessions) achieves cumulative volume improvement for HFM soft tissue deficiency.
